intro: Transitioning from TypeScript to Swift shifts development from a web-focused, single-threaded JavaScript ecosystem to Apple's highly compiled system language built for performance and safety. Developers migrate to Swift to build responsive, native applications across Apple platforms or create high-throughput Linux services. This change introduces ahead-of-time compilation, automatic reference counting, and compile-time type safety.

diffs.from: Static structural type checking that is erased during build phases, leaving execution fully dynamic.
diffs.to: Static nominal type layout with robust inference, checked ahead of time and retained via runtime metadata constraints.

diffs.from: C-style block syntax driven by lexical scopes, arrow closures, and object structural literals.
diffs.to: Clean modern nominal layout utilizing explicit argument labels, guard blocks, switch matching, and omission of semicolons.

diffs.from: Automated engine-level heap optimization driven by runtime generational garbage collection passes.
diffs.to: Deterministic Automatic Reference Counting (ARC) tracking object graphs, requiring weak references to prevent memory cycles.

diffs.from: Single-thread non-blocking architecture relying on microtask priority execution and native Promise variables.
diffs.to: Structured concurrency utilizing actor models, task groups, and modern async/await syntax mapped across system threads.

diffs.from: Minimal core structure requiring developers to pull a massive volume of external NPM utilities.
diffs.to: Comprehensive framework providing optimized collections, OS synchronization tools, file systems, and encoding structures natively.

examples.title: Protocol-Oriented Struct Definitions
examples.description: Contrasting a typescript structural type implementation against an idiomatic immutable Swift Struct utilizing explicit Protocols.

examples.title: Safe Optional Binding and Controls
examples.description: Replacing loose null-checks with explicit compile-time verified Swift unwrapping idioms.

tips.title: Prefer Structs Over Class Models
tips.body: Swift structures are value types passed by copying, which avoids implicit shared mutation bugs. Use classes only when reference lifecycles are required.

tips.title: Master Argument Identification Labels
tips.body: Swift requires explicit argument labels when invoking functions (e.g., loadData(for: userId)). Adjust function signatures to embrace this readability feature.

tips.title: Prevent Memory Leaks in Closures
tips.body: To stop reference retention cycles within escaping closures, declare explicit weak or unowned captures using '[weak self]' tracking syntax blocks.

tips.title: Enforce Early Boundaries with guard
tips.body: Use the 'guard' statement to unpack optional fields and handle early exits cleanly at the top of functions, which avoids nested 'if-let' blocks.

faqs.question: What balances the TypeScript structural union feature within Swift?
faqs.answer: Swift uses typed Enums with associated values to manage variable options safely, ensuring exhaustive compile-time verification during switch operations.

faqs.question: Can Swift code assets run on standard server systems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Swift contains robust Linux compilation flags and server frameworks like Vapor, enabling high-performance, safe backend web processing pipelines.

faqs.question: How are missing system parameters managed compared to JavaScript variables?
faqs.answer: Swift explicitly forbids standard unallocated references. Missing parameters use the 'Optional<Value>' enum, requiring compile-time validation to open.
